topic.what: Mastic gum (resin from Pistacia lentiscus) kills Helicobacter pylori in vitro at a minimum bactericidal concentration of 60 µg/mL, suggesting a natural antimicrobial agent for H. pylori-associated gastric disease.
topic.how: In-vitro bactericidal assay: serial dilutions of mastic gum extract incubated with H. pylori clinical isolates; viability assessed by culture; MBC determined at 60 µg/mL. Published as a letter in NEJM.
topic.why_matters: This landmark NEJM letter catalysed substantial research interest in mastic gum as a natural alternative or adjunct to antibiotic-based H. pylori eradication therapy, especially relevant given rising antibiotic resistance.

characteristics.dimension: Minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) against H. pylori
characteristics.what_it_measures: Lowest concentration of mastic gum extract that kills ≥99.9% of H. pylori in vitro
characteristics.evidence: Bactericidal MBC assay, H. pylori clinical isolates
characteristics.context: In-vitro only; gastric luminal concentrations after oral mastic gum ingestion not established in this paper

characteristics.what_it_measures: Activity against multiple H. pylori clinical strains
characteristics.value: Active against all tested clinical isolates
characteristics.context: Small isolate panel; strain diversity limited by letter format

good_for: Hypothesis generation for natural anti-H. pylori agents
Foundation for clinical trials of mastic gum in H. pylori gastritis
Research into plant-derived antimicrobials for antibiotic-resistant H. pylori
not_for: Direct clinical application without further in-vivo and RCT evidence
Replacement of established antibiotic eradication regimens based solely on this finding
Determining bioavailability or effective gastric luminal concentrations
requires: Follow-up clinical trials to confirm in-vivo bactericidal efficacy
Pharmacokinetic data on mastic gum components in gastric mucosa
Standardisation of mastic gum extract composition

novelty: First published demonstration that mastic gum kills H. pylori at low concentrations in vitro, published in one of the highest-impact medical journals
Initiated a new research direction linking Mediterranean traditional medicine to H. pylori management
Provided a tractable MBC value (60 µg/mL) that could be used as a benchmark for natural product screening
open_problems: Whether achievable luminal concentrations after oral dosing reach the 60 µg/mL MBC
Identification of the active antibacterial components within mastic gum resin
Clinical efficacy in RCTs — subsequent trials have had mixed results
Mechanism of bactericidal action against H. pylori
